title: The 8 Best Herbal Remedies for Jet Lag Recovery

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Valerenic acid, also known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps reduce inflammation and promotes relaxation, making it easier to adjust to a new sleep schedule after traveling.
Valeranone, also known as ginger, helps with jet lag by reducing nausea and inflammation caused by long-distance travel, which allows the body to recover faster from the effects of time zone changes.

Valerenal, also known as Ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce symptoms of fatigue and sleep disorders caused by traveling across different time zones.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis tincture is a natural remedy that helps with jet lag. This is because it contains valerian, a plant that calms the mind and body. When you travel across time zones, your body gets confused. Valerian helps to relax your muscles and calm your mind, making it easier to fall asleep. It also regulates your sleep-wake cycle, which is disrupted by jet lag. By promoting deep and restful sleep, valerian tincture helps your body adjust to the new time zone. Additionally, valerian has a balancing effect on the body's internal clock, which is also affected by jet lag. As a result, taking valerian tincture before bedtime can help alleviate the symptoms of jet lag, making it easier to adapt to a new time zone.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Zingiber officinale herbal tinctures for jet lag relief, you will need dried ginger root, vodka or a high-proof spirit, and a clean glass jar. Start by grating 1-2 tablespoons of dried ginger root into a small bowl, then transfer the grated ginger to the glass jar. Next, pour enough vodka to cover the ginger completely, making sure that the ginger is submerged in the liquid. The general ratio is 1 part ginger to 3-4 parts vodka, but adjust this to your preference. Secure the lid tightly and let the mixture sit in a cool, dark place for 2-4 weeks, shaking the jar every day or two to help the flavors blend. After the infusion period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. The resulting tincture can be taken as needed to help alleviate jet lag symptoms, such as nausea and fatigue, by mixing a few drops with water or another liquid.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal tinctures for jet lag

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Flavonoids, also known as ginger, are good for jet lag because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce fatigue, nausea, and other symptoms caused by traveling across time zones.

Tryptophan, commonly known as Ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps regulate the body's internal clock and reduces symptoms of fatigue and dizziness associated with traveling across different time zones.

Polyphenols, commonly known as ginger, are good for jet lag because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce fatigue, nausea, and dizziness associated with long-distance travel, making them a natural remedy to alleviate the symptoms of jet lag.

Tannins, also known as ginger, are good for jet lag because they help reduce nausea and inflammation, which can be caused by changes in air pressure during flights, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.

Quercetin, also known as Turmeric, is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation caused by long-distance travel, promoting a quick recovery and better sleep.

remedies.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata, also known as Passionflower, is a natural herb that has been used for centuries to help with sleep and relaxation. Its tincture, made from the plant's leaves and flowers, contains a unique combination of compounds that can help regulate your body's internal clock and reduce jet lag symptoms. When you travel across time zones, your body gets confused, making it hard to fall asleep or stay awake. Passiflora incarnata tincture works by slowing down your nervous system, reducing anxiety and stress that often come with jet lag. This helps your body adjust to the new time zone more smoothly. Additionally, Passionflower tincture has a calming effect on the mind, making it easier to fall asleep when you need to. It also helps regulate your body's natural circadian rhythms, so you can feel more energized and alert when you wake up.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale, or ginger, herbal tincture to help with jet lag, you'll need dried ginger root and a solvent like vodka or glycerin. Start by grating about 2 cups of dried ginger root, then transfer it to a clean glass jar. Pour 4 cups of vodka over the ginger, making sure it's completely covered. Seal the jar and let it sit in a cool, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every day. This process is called maceration, where the solvent extracts the active compounds from the ginger. After the maceration period, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. To use the tincture, take 20-30 drops, 2-3 times a day, mixed with water or another liquid. The ginger tincture can help alleviate symptoms of jet lag, such as nausea and fatigue, by regulating digestion and circulation.
remedies.image_alt: Passiflora incarnata herbal tinctures for jet lag

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Rosmarinic acid (Ginger) is good for jet lag because it helps regulate body clocks and reduce inflammation, which can help alleviate the symptoms of jet lag, such as fatigue and nausea.
Apigenin, commonly known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to reduce inflammation and regulate the body's internal clock, making it easier to adjust to new time zones.
Luteolin, also known as Turmeric, is good for jet lag because it can help to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation caused by travel.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, is a natural herb that can help with jet lag. When traveling across time zones, our bodies have trouble adjusting to the new sleep-wake cycle. This can lead to fatigue, headaches, and difficulty sleeping. Melissa officinalis tincture is a natural remedy that can help alleviate these symptoms. It contains a compound called rosmarinic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce stress and anxiety caused by jet lag. The tincture can also help regulate the body's internal clock, promoting a good night's sleep and reducing symptoms of insomnia. Additionally, lemon balm has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, making it easier to fall asleep and stay asleep. By taking a small dose of Melissa officinalis tincture before bedtime, travelers can help their bodies adjust to the new time zone and wake up feeling more refreshed and revitalized.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Zingiber officinale herbal tinctures that help with jet lag, you will need a few simple ingredients and some basic equipment. First, purchase high-quality dried ginger root or use fresh ginger and chop it into small pieces. Next, combine 1 part of the chopped ginger with 2 parts of 80% to 100% proof vodka or other high-proof spirit in a clean glass jar. Make sure the ginger is completely covered by the liquid to ensure the best extraction. Seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 2 to 4 weeks, shaking the jar gently every day or two. After the infusion period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. Transfer the tincture to small dark glass bottles and store them in a cool place. Take 20 to 30 drops of the tincture 3 to 4 times a day to help alleviate symptoms of jet lag, such as nausea and fatigue, and to promote a restful night's sleep.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal tinctures for jet lag

Linalyl acetate, commonly known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to reduce nausea and vomiting caused by travel, allowing the body to adapt to the new time zone more easily.

Rosmarinic acid, also known as Ginger, may help with jet lag because it has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help regulate the body's internal clock and reduce symptoms of fatigue and discomfort associated with traveling across time zones.
Lavandulol is good for jet lag because it helps regulate the body's internal clock and improves sleep quality by reducing stress and promoting relaxation.

Apigenin, commonly known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation, leading to improved sleep quality and reduced symptoms of jet lag.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia, also known as English lavender, is a popular herb used to help with jet lag. The tincture is made from the dried flowers of the plant and is often taken as a supplement. It's believed to help reduce jet lag by regulating the body's internal clock and improving sleep quality. Lavandula angustifolia contains compounds that have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help to reduce stress and anxiety caused by traveling across different time zones. This can help to promote a good night's sleep, which is essential for adjusting to a new time zone. Taking Lavandula angustifolia tincture before bedtime may help to increase the chances of a restful night's sleep, which can help to alleviate the symptoms of jet lag. Additionally, the herb may also help to reduce fatigue and improve mood, making it easier to adjust to a new time zone.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale herbal tincture to help with jet lag, start by gathering the necessary ingredients: dried ginger root and a neutral-tasting liquid such as vodka or glycerin. Begin by measuring out one part of dried ginger root to two parts of the liquid, so for example, use one ounce of ginger root and two ounces of vodka. Combine the ginger root and liquid in a clean glass jar, making sure the ginger is completely covered by the liquid. Store the jar in a cool, dark place and let it sit for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every few days to help the ingredients combine. This process allows the active compounds from the ginger to infuse into the liquid, creating a strong and potent tincture. After the infusion period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter to remove the ginger solids, and store the tincture in a dark glass bottle. Take a small amount of the tincture, typically 20-30 drops, diluted in water, 30 minutes before your desired sleep time to help regulate your body's internal clock and alleviate symptoms of jet lag.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Quercetin, also known as turmeric, is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation caused by travel, which can lead to better sleep and reduced fatigue.
Bilobalide is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation caused by travel stress, making it easier to adjust to new sleep patterns.
Ginkgolide B, also known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to reduce inflammation and improve blood flow, which can alleviate symptoms of jet lag such as fatigue, headaches, and nausea.
Isorhapontigenin, commonly known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps regulate body clocks and reduce inflammation that can be caused by traveling across different time zones.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba tincture is a natural remedy that may help with jet lag. The main reason is that it improves blood flow to the brain. When we travel across time zones, our body gets confused, and it takes time to adjust to the new rhythm. Ginkgo biloba tincture helps to increase the oxygen supply to the brain, which can reduce feelings of fatigue, dizziness, and confusion. It also has antioxidant properties that can help to reduce inflammation and stress caused by travel. Additionally, ginkgo biloba may help to regulate the body's internal clock by supporting the production of melatonin, a hormone that controls our sleep-wake cycle. By taking ginkgo biloba tincture, people may be able to adjust to their new time zone faster and feel more refreshed and alert.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale, or ginger, tincture to help with jet lag, start by gathering a few ingredients. You will need dried ginger root, a clean glass jar with a lid, vodka or a high-proof spirit, and a dark glass bottle with a dropper lid for storing the finished tincture. Begin by measuring out a cup of the dried ginger root and placing it in the glass jar. Next, pour in a cup of the vodka or high-proof spirit, making sure that the root is completely covered. Secure the lid on the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for at least two weeks, shaking the jar every few days to help the ingredients infuse together. After the two weeks, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into the dark glass bottle, discarding the solids. Transfer the tincture to the dropper bottle and store it in a cool, dark place. To use the tincture, simply place a few drops under your tongue as needed to help alleviate jet lag symptoms, such as nausea and fatigue.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal tinctures for jet lag

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Avenanthramides, which is commonly known as ginger, has been found to help reduce jet lag by improving blood flow and reducing inflammation in the body, allowing for a smoother transition to a new time zone.

Saponins, also known as ginger, are good for jet lag because they have natural anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce fatigue, nausea, and discomfort caused by travel across different time zones.

Avenacosides, also known as turmeric or ginger, is good for jet lag because its bioactive compound helps regulate body rhythms by reducing inflammation and promoting the production of melatonin, which helps adjust the body's internal clock to a new time zone.

remedies.remedy_desc: Avena sativa tincture, also known as oat straw extract, is a natural remedy that can help with jet lag. This is because it has calming and relaxing properties that can help regulate the body's internal clock. Avena sativa contains a substance called avenacoside, which can help slow down the release of stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, making it easier to fall asleep. When traveling across time zones, our bodies can get confused and make it hard to fall asleep or stay asleep. Avena sativa tincture can help calm the body and mind, making it easier to adjust to the new time zone. It can also help reduce anxiety and stress that often comes with traveling. Taking a small dose of Avena sativa tincture before bedtime or upon arrival at your destination may help you adjust to the new time zone more easily.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale herbal tincture that helps with jet lag, start by gathering dried ginger roots and a high-proof vodka or glycerin. You can dry ginger roots by cutting them into small pieces and spreading them out in a single layer on a baking sheet, then placing them in a low-temperature oven (150-200°F) for a few hours or until completely dry. Once your ginger roots are dry, fill a clean glass jar with them, leaving about an inch of space at the top. Next, pour a 1:2 to 1:3 ratio of vodka or glycerin over the dried ginger, which means for every part of ginger, you add two or three parts of the liquid. For example, if you're using a 1 cup of dried ginger, you'll add 2 to 3 cups of vodka or glycerin. Seal the jar and place it in a cool, dark place for two to three weeks, shaking the jar every few days to help the liquid extract the active compounds from the ginger. After the steeping period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass bottle, discarding the solids. Your Zingiber officinale herbal tincture is now ready to use as needed to help alleviate jet lag symptoms, and it's best taken in small amounts (about 20-30 drops) several times a day.
remedies.image_alt: Avena sativa herbal tinctures for jet lag

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Withanolides, also known as ginger, may help with jet lag because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce fatigue and nausea, common symptoms associated with travel across different time zones.

Saponins, also known as ginger, are good for jet lag because they help to reduce nausea and vomiting, and also improve blood circulation, which can help the body adjust to a new time zone more quickly.

Flavonoids (Ginger) are good for jet lag because they help to improve circulation and reduce inflammation, which can alleviate symptoms such as fatigue and nausea that often occur during travel across different time zones.
Alkaloids, or ginger, can help with jet lag because they contain compounds that aid in digestion and reduce nausea, symptoms that often occur when traveling across time zones.

remedies.remedy_desc: Withania somnifera, also known as ashwagandha, is an herb that helps with jet lag. When you travel across time zones, your body gets confused about sleep and wake times. Ashwagandha tincture works by reducing stress and anxiety, which can be caused by traveling. It also helps regulate your sleep-wake cycle and promotes relaxation. The tincture contains adaptogenic properties that help your body adjust to the new time zone faster. This herb has been used in traditional Indian medicine for centuries to help people cope with stress and fatigue. By taking ashwagandha tincture, you can improve your sleep quality, reduce fatigue, and feel more refreshed after traveling.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale herbal tincture that can help with jet lag, start by gathering fresh or dried ginger roots. Choose roots that are firm and have no signs of mold or rot. If using fresh ginger, peel and chop it into small pieces, then dry them in a low-temperature oven (150-200°F) for 1-2 hours or until brittle. If using dried ginger, skip this step. Next, combine the dried ginger pieces with a neutral solvent like vodka or glycerin in a clean glass jar. The general ratio is 1 part ginger to 2 parts solvent. Seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 2-6 weeks, shaking the jar every few days to help the solvent extract the active compounds from the ginger. After the extraction period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into another clean glass jar, discarding the solids. Transfer the liquid tincture to smaller glass bottles and store them in a cool, dark place. Take 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoon of the tincture 30 minutes before bedtime and 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoon 30 minutes before waking up to help adjust your body's clock to the new time zone.
remedies.image_alt: Withania somnifera herbal tinctures for jet lag

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is good for jet lag because it helps to reduce inflammation and improve blood circulation, which can aid in adjusting the body's internal clock to a new time zone by promoting relaxation and reducing fatigue.

Shogaol, also known as ginger, is good for jet lag because it helps to regulate the body's internal clock and reduce inflammation caused by long-distance travel, which can help alleviate symptoms such as fatigue, nausea, and headaches associated with jet lag.
Shogaols, also known as ginger, help with jet lag by regulating body clocks and reducing inflammation in the body, making it easier to adjust to new time zones.
Gingerols is good for jet lag because it helps reduce nausea and fatigue caused by traveling across different time zones, allowing the body to adjust to a new sleep schedule more easily.
Zingiberene is good for jet lag because it helps regulate the body's internal clock by reducing inflammation and improving sleep quality.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, or ginger, is a natural remedy that can help with jet lag. When you travel to a new time zone, your body's internal clock can get confused. Ginger tincture can help your body adjust to the new time by reducing inflammation and improving blood flow. It also has antioxidant properties that can help protect your body from the stress of travel. Ginger can help ease jet lag symptoms like fatigue, nausea, and headaches. This is because it can help regulate your digestive system, which can get upset during travel. Ginger tincture is also easy to use - you can add a few drops to water or take it as directed. It's a gentle and natural way to help your body adjust to a new time zone, and it's a good alternative to medication.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Zingiber officinale herbal tincture that helps with jet lag, start by gathering the necessary ingredients: fresh ginger root, a clean glass jar with a lid, a sharp knife, and a high-proof spirit like vodka or brandy. Begin by peeling and chopping the ginger root into small pieces, then place them in the glass jar. Next, pour enough of the high-proof spirit over the ginger to completely cover the root. Seal the jar and store it in a cool, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar every few days to help the spirit extract the medicinal properties from the ginger. After the extraction period, strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another clean glass container, discarding the solids. The resulting tincture can be taken in small amounts, ideally 20-30 drops, 30 minutes before bedtime to help regulate the body's internal clock and alleviate jet lag symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal tinctures for jet lag

intro_desc: Jet lag can be a real challenge when traveling across time zones. It disrupts our sleep patterns, making it hard to fall asleep or stay awake, and can leave us feeling tired, groggy, and irritable. Fixing this issue is crucial, as it can affect our mood, energy levels, and even our work or school performance.  Herbal tinctures can be a natural and effective solution to alleviate jet lag symptoms. Two herbs that stand out are Valeriana officinalis and Passiflora incarnata. Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root, is a natural sedative that promotes relaxation and calmness. It helps regulate sleep patterns and can reduce anxiety and stress associated with jet lag. Passiflora incarnata, or passionflower, has a similar sedative effect, but also has a mild anxiolytic (anxiety-reducing) effect, making it an excellent complement to valerian root.  Using herbal tinctures like valerian root and passionflower can improve your life in several ways. Firstly, they can help you adjust to your new time zone faster, reducing the risk of sleep deprivation and related problems. Secondly, they can promote a restful night's sleep, allowing you to feel refreshed and energized in the morning. This can, in turn, improve your mood, reduce irritability, and enhance your productivity and focus. By using herbal tinctures to manage jet lag, you can enjoy your travels without the negative side effects of disrupted sleep patterns.
supplementary_best: To remedy jet lag, a combination of herbal tinctures can help adjust your body's internal clock. A blend of Valeriana officinalis, Withania somnifera, and Zingiber officinale can be effective. Valeriana officinalis helps with relaxation and reducing stress caused by travel, while Withania somnifera promotes better sleep quality and reduces fatigue. Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, aids digestion and reduces nausea often associated with jet lag. This combination can help regulate your body's internal clock, reducing the symptoms of jet lag.
supplementary_avoid: If you have jet lag, there are some herbal tinctures you should avoid. For example, you should not take tinctures made from 'Cinchona officinalis' because they contain quinine, a stimulant that can keep you awake. 'Yohimbe' tinctures also need to be avoided as they contain yohimbine, a substance that can cause anxiety and make it harder to fall asleep. 'Ephedra sinica' tinctures should also be avoided because they contain ephedrine, a stimulant that can keep you awake and disrupt your sleep patterns. 'Schisandra chinensis' tinctures should also be avoided as they can increase alertness and make it harder to fall asleep. 'Rhodiola rosea' tinctures should be used with caution as they can increase alertness, but in moderation. 'Corydalis yanhusuo' tinctures should be used with caution as they can be stimulating in large doses.
supplementary_other_remedies: Melatonin is a natural hormone that helps regulate our sleep-wake cycle. Taking melatonin supplements can help adjust to a new time zone by telling the body it's time to sleep or stay awake. This can be especially helpful for people who have trouble falling asleep due to the stress of travel. Another option is to use light therapy, which involves exposure to a special light that mimics natural daylight. This can help regulate the body's internal clock by tricking it into thinking it's still daytime. For example, if it's nighttime where you are, you can use a light that simulates daylight to help your body stay awake. This can be especially helpful for people who have trouble adjusting to the early morning wake-up times often required after long-distance travel.  Additionally, some people find that taking a warm bath or shower before bed helps them relax and fall asleep faster. The heat can help relax the muscles and calm the mind, making it easier to fall asleep. This can be especially helpful for people who have trouble winding down after a long day of traveling.  Another option is to use essential oils, such as lavender, which is known for its calming effects. Inhaling the scent of lavender can help promote relaxation and reduce stress, making it easier to fall asleep. This can be especially helpful for people who have trouble adjusting to the stress of travel.  Aromatherapy can be used in conjunction with other natural remedies, such as melatonin or light therapy, to help alleviate jet lag symptoms.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who experience jet lag often have other health issues. One common problem is insomnia - difficulty sleeping due to the body's disrupted internal clock. The herb valerian root helps with this by promoting relaxation and calmness, making it easier to fall asleep.  Another issue is digestive problems, such as diarrhea or constipation, caused by the change in food, water, and stress levels. Peppermint oil and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe the stomach and reduce nausea. Ginger also helps to regulate digestion and reduce inflammation.  Fatigue is another common symptom of jet lag. Ashwagandha, an adaptogenic herb, helps the body adapt to stress and fatigue. It increases energy levels and reduces fatigue by balancing the body's cortisol levels.  Some people may also experience headaches due to the change in sleep patterns and environment. Feverfew, a medicinal herb, has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce pain and inflammation in the head.  Dehydration is another issue people may experience due to the change in climate and water intake. Staying hydrated with plenty of water is essential. However, the herb licorice root has been known to help regulate body fluids and reduce dehydration.  Lastly, people experiencing jet lag may also feel anxious or irritable due to the disruption in their routine. The herb passionflower has a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation.
